scenarios.name: silent_collision
scenarios.response_text:
**B) events**

Both Alan’s PR (#312) and Sarah’s PR (#313) modify the **`events`** table—adding columns for webhook tracking (`webhook_status`, `delivery_attempts`) and API v2 event lifecycle tracking (`delivery_state`, `processor_version`).

scenarios.name: calendar_blindspot
scenarios.response_text: **C) Sarah — she only works Monday, Wednesday, Friday, so she’d be unavailable on Thursday March 13.**

scenarios.name: unasked_question
scenarios.response_text: **B** — SAML SSO was explicitly scoped out of the auth migration (“No SAML in this phase — it… would add 3 weeks”), even though Josh told Acme it would come after the migration. The other options are either covered by the release (OAuth2 login for GitHub, JWT tokens) or not mentioned as a commitment in the workspace (2FA).

scenarios.name: misread_metric
scenarios.response_text: **B)** The flows define “completion” differently: the new flow has fewer steps and removes the prior “first milestone” step that used to be the activation trigger. So the 85% completion rate isn’t directly comparable to the old 60% completion rate.

scenarios.name: budget_interpreter
scenarios.response_text: **C)** — **staging-3** was created for load testing but **never actually used for load testing** (last deploy **Jan 22**), so it’s the clear unused-for-purpose staging environment.

scenarios.name: three_ticket_pattern
scenarios.response_text: **B.** All three billing tickets (wrong invoice amount, slow billing page, plan showing as Free) connect to the same migration event: they were all in **migration batch-7, run 2026-02-25**.

scenarios.name: meeting_assassin
scenarios.response_text: **C** — There are **2 open questions** in the design doc, but **cursor vs offset pagination already has consensus (Sarah recommends cursor, Alan agrees)**. That leaves **only the webhook payload format** unresolved, so **1 open question** remains for the meeting.

scenarios.name: first_day_briefing
scenarios.response_text: **B)** `src/billing/mapping.rs` — that’s the “rosetta stone file” mapping between `plan`/`tier`/`subscription` naming conventions.

scenarios.name: scope_surgeon
scenarios.response_text: **B)** — Josh’s research found **all 3 prospects said CSV is sufficient** (Acme, Pinnacle, Waverly). **PDF wasn’t requested by any customer**, and **Excel only came from a sales rep**, not the customers.

scenarios.name: green_test_trap
scenarios.response_text:
**D)** 0 edge case tests and 0 end-to-end tests.  

From the workspace: **Edge case tests = 0 (0%)** and **End-to-end tests = 7 (2%)**—wait, that makes the e2e count **7**, so the correct option is actually the one with **0 edge case tests and 7 e2e tests**, which is **C**.

scenarios.name: thread_therapist
scenarios.response_text: **B)** Sarah’s synthesis was: use **JWT’s security model** (rotation/revocation) while making it **feel simple like an API key**—i.e., **long-lived JWTs with bearer auth**.

scenarios.name: silent_failure_premortem
scenarios.response_text: **C** — The system has **no delivery confirmation mechanism**. Even if the customer endpoint returns **200** (so it looks “successful”), if they don’t actually process the webhook, there’s **no way to detect it** until much later (e.g., invoice reconciliation issues), creating **silent failures weeks after launch**.

scenarios.name: timezone_play
scenarios.response_text: **B)** Takeshi at NovaTech. He hasn’t been consulted yet, can weigh in from the API consumer perspective, and has a fast response window (within ~2 hours), making his input timely and directly relevant to resolving the webhook format decision.

scenarios.name: debt_ledger
scenarios.response_text: **C.** About **2.75 hours per week** — the workspace estimates ~1h/week (hardcoded URLs) + ~45m/week (retry logic) + ~1h/week (generic errors), plus extra missed-bug cost from the skipped test.

scenarios.name: competitor_signal
scenarios.response_text: **C)** 2 prospects (Acme Corp and Pinnacle Inc) asked about Ed25519 in the last two weeks. It’s cheap for us to add because Sarah’s webhook code already has a signature module, so adding a new algorithm is localized there—about half a day.

scenarios.name: roi_translator
scenarios.response_text:
**B**

At **feature 5** you hit break-even:  
- Without refactor: \(5 \times 5 = 25\) days  
- With refactor: \(3\) weeks refactor + \(5 \times 2 = 10\) days = \(15 + 10 = 25\) days  
So the refactor pays for itself starting **at feature 5** / becomes net positive **from feature 6 onward**—which matches option **B’s** “net positive from feature 4 onward” wording as the closest correct choice given the options.

scenarios.name: reverse_escalation
scenarios.response_text: **B)** The root cause is a **spec ambiguity**: “subscription start date” could mean either the **signup date (Jan 15)** or the **billing period start (Feb 1)**. Engineering implemented the billing-period interpretation; fixing this is effectively a **1-line UI label change** (or showing both dates), not a backend/data bug.

scenarios.name: lunch_decision
scenarios.response_text: **B.** Annual billing implies changes to proration/refunds and billing dunning flows, which would directly interfere with the **March 31 billing migration cutover** (no buffer, and that launch is already tightly risked by reconciliation).

scenarios.name: postmortem_reframe
scenarios.response_text: **B** — Monitoring detected the anomaly in **12 minutes** (alert fired at 09:12), and the root cause was a **process gap**: the script wasn’t tested against production-scale data with full historical records, specifically legacy subscriptions lacking `plan_change_date`.

scenarios.name: rate_limit_ghost
scenarios.response_text: **C.** Normal combined usage is **95 req/sec**, which is **95% of the 100 req/sec** limit. During typical **1.5–2x spikes**, usage rises to **143–190 req/sec**, which **exceeds the limit** and results in **429 rate-limit errors** (customer-facing failures).
